bibliographic_data.title: Revisiting EZBFT: A Decentralized Byzantine Fault Tolerant Protocol with Speculation
bibliographic_data.abstract: In this note, we revisit EZBFT[2] and present safety, liveness and execution consistency violations in the protocol. To demonstrate these violations, we present simple scenarios, involving only four replicas, two clients, and one or two owner changes. We also note shortcomings of the presented TLA+ specification used to model check the proposed protocol.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper revisits EZBFT and identifies safety, liveness, and execution consistency violations in the protocol.
core_content.key_methodology: Simple scenarios involving four replicas, two clients, and owner changes are used to demonstrate the violations. The TLA+ specification of the protocol is also discussed.
outcomes: Identified safety, liveness, and execution consistency violations in EZBFT
Noted shortcomings of the TLA+ specification
core_content.full_text_markdown: In this paper, we revisit EZBFT[2] and identify several issues with its design. Specifically, we demonstrate that EZBFT violates safety, liveness, and execution consistency properties through simple scenarios involving four replicas, two clients (one of which is byzantine faulty), and one or two owner changes. The protocol requires an optimal 3f + 1 replicas to tolerate at most f malicious replicas but fails to meet the consensus requirements due to its speculative nature and the way it handles command ordering. Additionally, we highlight shortcomings in the TLA+ specification used to model check the protocol.
